result:
The first thing that arrives is the scent of rain on hot concrete—petrichor, the textbooks say, but that word always feels too clinical for the way the sky seems to exhale memory. I’m eight again, barefoot on the porch, believing the hiss of water evaporating from the driveway is the sound of the sun being quenched. I’m also thirty-eight, in a city that has forgotten how to keep its promises, watching the same hiss rise from a different street while my phone buzzes with news I don’t want to read. Time folds like an origami crane, sharp and delicate; the beak of the bird bites both versions of me at once.

I keep thinking about the edge of things. The lip of a coffee cup, the horizon line, the final sentence of a book you already know will wreck you and you read anyway. Edges are where possibility leaks out—physicists call it quantum tunneling, lovers call it maybe. I once stood on the rim of the Grand Canyon at dawn, expecting grandeur, but what stunned me was the silence: a hush so complete it felt like the planet had paused mid-breath. A raven landed on a guardrail not three feet away, matte black against matte black sky, and when it cocked its head I swear it was asking, “Are you coming or going?” I didn’t have an answer then; I still don’t. Sometimes I wake up in the dark and feel the canyon’s vacuum in my rib cage, a draft that whistles through the bars of my sternum like a child learning to mourn.

Language fails most when you need it most. My father had a stroke last year; the word “water” came out “river,” then “silver,” then nothing. He wept at the frustration of it, a man who once recited entire verses of Neruda to the dog because the dog listened better than any human. Now we sit in the living room that used to smell of cedar and cigar smoke, and the air is thick with the unsaid. I bring him glasses of water—actual water, no metaphors—and he drinks like he’s swallowing the syllables he lost. After he naps, I open my notebook and try to write him back into himself: the scar on his shoulder shaped like Chile, the way he hummed “Girl from Ipanema” while shaving. The pen wobbles; ink pools into miniature oceans. I realize I’m not trying to save him; I’m trying to save the version of me that still has a father who remembers my middle name.

There’s a theory that every choice spawns a parallel universe. Somewhere, I turned left instead of right, missed the train, never met the woman who would teach me grief is just another word between “gravity” and “gravy.” In that universe my phone isn’t buzzing with headlines about glaciers calving like slow-motion thunder. In that universe I still believe the world ends with a bang instead of a thousand tiny cuts delivered by “update” notifications. But here, in this branch of the multiverse, I watch a starling murmuration tilt above the courthouse and for thirty seconds the city forgets to be cruel. The birds write cursive against the dusk: Not everything is lost. Then they settle into the poplars and the spell breaks; sirens reassert their minor key.

I read once that the human heart can keep beating even after it’s removed from the body, as long as it has oxygen. I think about that fact more than is probably healthy—how the muscle that keeps us alive is also the most desperate fugitive. Mine left my chest the day my cat got hit by a car and the vet handed me a shoebox saying, “She’s in a better place,” as if better exists anywhere outside the warmth of my pulse against her fur. I buried the box under the lemon tree, and every winter when the tree fruits, the lemons taste faintly of ash. I make lemonade anyway, add too much sugar, invite the neighbors. We stand in the yard sipping memory, politely pretending the tartness isn’t grief.

Tonight the moon is a paper cutout taped to the sky. I walk to the corner store for nothing in particular; the cashier recognizes me, puts the change in my palm like she’s returning something I’d loaned her. On the way back I spot a dandelion forcing its way through a crack in the asphalt—defiant, stupid, magnificent. I kneel, take a photo, then delete it. Some things are meant to be witnessed, not archived. The wind shifts; pollen drifts onto my sleeve like tiny suns. I blow once, half-hearted, and the seeds scatter into the dark. I don’t make a wish. I’ve run out of plausible futures to bargain for.

Back home I open the window and the room fills with the sound of someone else’s party: laughter in 4/4 time, a bassline that rattles the glass. I sit on the floor among piles of unmailed letters. Each envelope contains a version of an apology I keep revising: to the friend I ghosted, to the student I underestimated, to the stranger on the train whose name I asked and immediately forgot. Stamps are expensive, but regret is cheap and compounds daily. I imagine the postman finding them after I die—wonders whether to deliver or discard. Maybe he’ll read them aloud to his kid, who will grow up to be a writer, and one day she’ll win an award for a story titled The Year My Father Brought Home Someone Else’s Shame. She’ll thank the unknown dead man in the acknowledgments. I’ll be a footnote in someone else’s healing; that’s more than most ghosts get.

Midnight arrives wearing the face of a clock I’ve never bothered to set. I brew tea strong enough to stain the spoon. The steam curls into the shape of mountains I will never climb. I think about how “good night” is just “goodbye” wearing softer clothes. I say it to the walls, to the neighbor’s cat staring from the fire escape, to the reflection in the window that looks like me if I’d made better choices. None of us answer, but the kettle whistles, a small bright anthem. I drink. The heat fills the canyon inside me for approximately thirty seconds. It’s enough to get to tomorrow, and sometimes that’s what counts—one more cup, one more dandelion, one more syllable closer to river.
